changeReason: New translation adds "road" ("a long road"), introducing a journey metaphor not present in the Japanese, which simply says 長かった ("it was long").

TextENGNew: Are all the pieces we could want now assembled on the game board?
In the game of fate that turns on June 1983, have we gathered enough pieces to defeat Takano and her allies?
changeReason: "her minions" implies subordinate underlings while "her allies" implies cooperative partners; 鷹野たち simply means "Takano and the others," so the characterization differs.
Old says "we have everything" (we possess all needed items) while new says "they have" (the pieces have assembled), changing the subject and framing of who/what has lined up.

TextENGNew: All that remains is you, Hanyuu.

changeReason: Old version ("it's you") omits the exclusivity conveyed by だけ ("only"); the new version "Only you" correctly emphasizes that Hanyuu alone remains.

TextENGNew: The time you can appear as a piece has already grown quite limited.
But that does not mean you can no longer take part in the game.
changeReason: "show up" means merely appearing, while "take part" means actively participating; the distinction changes what capability is being preserved for Hanyuu.

TextENGNew: After all, I have wagered hundreds of years for this very day.
changeReason: "put in" implies time invested, while "wagered" (matching Japanese 賭けた) implies gambling/risking something — fundamentally different framing of what those hundreds of years meant.
